message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background and objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Ecological studies indicate ambient particulate matter ≤2.5mm (PM
                    <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                    ) air pollution is associated with poorer COVID-19 outcomes. However, these studies cannot account for individual heterogeneity and often have imprecise estimates of PM
                    <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                    exposure. We review evidence from studies using individual-level data to determine whether PM
                    <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                    increases risk of COVID-19 infection, severe disease, and death.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Systematic review of case-control and cohort studies, searching Medline, Embase, and WHO COVID-19 up to 30 June 2022. Study quality was evaluated using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale. Results were pooled with a random effects meta-analysis, with Egger’s regression, funnel plots, and leave-one-out and trim-and-fill analyses to adjust for publication bias.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                    =18 studies met inclusion criteria. A 10μg/m3 increase in PM
                    <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                    exposure was associated with 66% (95% CI: 1.31-2.11) greater odds of COVID-19 infection (N=7) and 127% (95% CI: 1.41-3.66) increase in severe illness (hospitalisation or worse) (N=6). Pooled mortality results (N=5) were positive but non-significant (OR 1.40; 0.94 to 2.10). Most studies were rated “good” quality (14/18 studies), though there were numerous methodological issues; few used individual-level data to adjust for confounders like socioeconomic status (4/18 studies), instead using area-based indicators (12/18 studies) or not adjusting for it (3/18 studies). Most severity (9/10 studies) and mortality studies (5/6 studies) were based on people already diagnosed COVID-19, potentially introducing collider bias.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    There is strong evidence that ambient PM
                    <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                    increases the risk of COVID-19 infection, and weaker evidence of increases in severe disease and mortality.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This review was completed as a Scholarly Intensive Placement project by NS, which received no funding.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Competing interests</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The authors declare no competing interests.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    This study was registered on PROSPERO on 8 July 2022 (CRD42022345129):
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/prospero/display_record.php?ID=CRD42022345129">https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/prospero/display_record.php?ID=CRD42022345129</jats:ext-link>
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Particulate matter air pollution and COVID-19 infection, severity, and mortality: A systematic review
